items.title: Declarations, prototypes and definitions: reject the invalid rule
items.prompt: Which statement correctly resolves this C misconception? Writing reset() in a C declaration always means the function takes no arguments, exactly like reset(void).
hints: Compare each option with the exact language rule, standard boundary, and observable evidence in the authored draft.

feedback.correct: Correct. In a C prototype, use (void) to declare no parameters; an empty parameter list does not provide the same prototype information.
feedback.incorrect: That option preserves the documented misconception. In a C prototype, use (void) to declare no parameters; an empty parameter list does not provide the same prototype information.

criteria.description: Selects the source-aligned C correction instead of the documented misconception.

options.text: In a C prototype, use (void) to declare no parameters; an empty parameter list does not provide the same prototype information.
Writing reset() in a C declaration always means the function takes no arguments, exactly like reset(void).

answer.explanation: In a C prototype, use (void) to declare no parameters; an empty parameter list does not provide the same prototype information.

items.title: Declarations, prototypes and definitions: executable application
